project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase the utilization and quality of health, nutrition, and family planning services
project_overview.total_financing: US$600 million (IDA Grant: US$140 million; ARTF: US$425 million; GFF: US$35 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Entire population of Afghanistan (29.7 million), with disproportionate benefits to poor people, rural populations, and women and children

primary_locations: 3 provinces (Parwan, Kapisa, Panjshir) with MOPH-Strengthening Mechanism
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - partial. Document notes: 'Between January and September 2017, 2,640 people were killed and around 5,380 injured. The number of Internally Displaced People has increased at an alarming pace: in the first half of 2017 alone, more than 200,000 people have been displaced internally due to conflict and more than 296,000 refugees have returned from Iran and Pakistan.' The Afghanistan Health Services Study found 'remarkable resilience of health service delivery in conflict-affected areas' with no statistically significant differences in improvements between severely, moderately, and minimally conflict-affected provinces. Security is noted as a critical challenge, especially in southern and eastern provinces.

key_components.description: Performance-based contracts to deliver Basic Package of Health Services (BPHS) and Essential Package of Hospital Services (EPHS) in 31 provinces. Includes combined BPHS/EPHS contracts, MOPH-Strengthening Mechanism in 3 provinces, urban BPHS in Kabul. Introduces fee-for-service and lump-sum contracting with increased managerial autonomy. Supports innovations in service access, utilization, household engagement, and quality. Expands Primary Health Centers (PHCs) in underserved provinces using geographic information system analysis.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening the Health System and its Performance
key_components.description: Supports implementation of functional review recommendations. Finances third-party monitoring (TPM) for annual health facility surveys using balanced scorecard methodology, six-monthly HMIS verification, household surveys, and drug quality verification. Provides technical assistance for capacity building in performance management, data analysis, and HMIS strengthening. Finances program management, incremental operating costs, and contractual techno-managerial staff (grades A-C) at central and provincial levels, capped at 1% of project value (US$6 million).

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Strengthening Demand and Community Accountability for Key Health Services
key_components.description: Enhances coverage of maternal health, family planning, and nutrition services through Citizens' Charter Afghanistan Program (CCAP) and Community Development Councils (CDCs). Finances social and behavioral change communications (SBCC) campaigns on health rights, maternal health, nutrition, and family planning. Strengthens community accountability through community scorecards and health information system integration. Strengthens grievance redress mechanism (GRM) through dissemination, SOP improvement, and semiannual reviews. Supports implementation of MOPH anti-corruption strategy.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Public Health (MOPH), with Ministry of Finance as borrower
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: High-Level Health Program Oversight Committee consisting of MOPH, Ministry of Finance, Independent Directorate of Local Governance, UN agencies, bilateral donors, and civil society representatives. Sehatmandi Coordination Office (renamed from SEHAT Coordination Office) provides secretariat support and coordinates with all stakeholders. Minister of Public Health serves as project coordinator. Provincial Health Offices (PHOs) and Technical Departments (TDs) involved in oversight and performance management.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Quality- and Cost-Based Selection for service provider contracts. Direct procurement by MOPH's Grants and Contract Management Unit (GCMU) for consulting firms, individual consultants, and non-consulting services. Shift from consultancy to non-consultancy contracts for service providers in selected provinces. Procurement probity assurance providers engaged. Simplified Project Procurement Strategy Document (PPSD) used due to emergency operation status. Procurement Plan disclosed on MOPH and World Bank websites, updated annually.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Multi-layered M&E system: (1) Health Management Information System (HMIS) providing real-time utilization data with quarterly assessment and six-monthly third-party verification; (2) Third-party monitoring conducting annual health facility assessments using balanced scorecard, household surveys, and drug quality verification; (3) Community-based monitoring through Community Development Councils using community scorecards; (4) Systematic supervision by Provincial Health Offices and Technical Departments; (5) Semiannual performance reviews of BPHS/EPHS service providers; (6) Household health surveys coordinated with Demographic Health Survey. Baseline from Afghanistan Health Survey 2018 and balanced scorecard. Semiannual reports on progress and sector performance. World Bank supervision missions every six months.

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple sources of high political and governance risks: power-sharing arrangements under National Unity Government create tension and instability; elections scheduled for 2019 could cause political disruptions; concerns about corruption in health sector; weak oversight and lack of planned approach to service provider contract management; political interference and micromanagement in service provider activities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Move fast initially with 'quick win' areas before elections; pilot non-consultancy services approach for procurement; develop SOPs for enhanced performance management; implement MOPH anti-corruption strategy; functional review of MOPH to clarify roles and responsibilities; orientation of provincial authorities on project modality; inclusion of development partners in evaluation committees; engagement of procurement probity assurance providers

identified_risks.risk_description: General security situation is critical challenge, especially in southern and eastern provinces. Between January-September 2017, 2,640 people killed and 5,380 injured. Over 200,000 internally displaced in first half of 2017. Security affects business confidence and economic activity. Increased conflict eroding development gains.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Lessons learned from ongoing SEHAT operation emphasize close work with local communities. Project design based on 15 years of experience in conflict-affected areas. Afghanistan Health Services Study found remarkable resilience of health service delivery in conflict-affected areas with no significant differences in improvements between conflict-affected provinces. Community engagement and accountability mechanisms strengthen local ownership.

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan's macroeconomic outlook subject to high risks. Country heavily reliant on aid; any reduction in security and civilian support below expected levels will pressure fiscal sustainability and service delivery. Economic growth projected at 2.6% in 2017, 3.2% in 2018, and 3.6% by 2020 - significantly below 9.6% average between 2003-2012. Poverty rate increased from 36% (2011/12) to 39.1% (2013/14). Unemployment at 22.6%, particularly severe for women and low-skilled workers.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bulk of financing from ARTF and IDA reduces macroeconomic risk to project. Project does not depend heavily on Government counterpart funding. Focus on labor-intensive and community-based programs that directly reach poorest populations. Fiscal sustainability analysis indicates project allocations would not create undue burden on health sector budget.

identified_risks.risk_description: Institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability rated substantial risk. Public sector characterized by highly uneven and thinly spread technical capacity. Long-term aid dependency has left agencies and reform processes heavily dependent on technical assistance. Weak internal audit lacking technical capacity and institutional independence. Poor maintenance of records and asset inventory. Fragmented data sets across different technical departments.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports strengthening of health system and increasing efficiency through functional review implementation. Contractual staff in key departments to support contract management and service delivery. Annual performance review of contractual staff with decreasing reliance over time as civil service reform picks up. Technical assistance for capacity building in performance management, data analysis, and HMIS. Development of SOPs and training for audit functions. Asset management software development. Engagement of firm for data warehouse development and capacity building.

identified_risks.risk_description: Fiduciary risk rating substantial. FM assessment identified weaknesses: absence of periodic financial reporting to management; delays in provincial financial reports; unadjusted advances for over a year; lack of asset management system; poor internal audit; noncompliance with external audit recommendations; incremental budget preparation approach. Procurement risks include unclear terms of reference, subjective evaluation criteria, lack of meaningful partner involvement, weak conflict of interest mechanisms. Concerns about corruption in health sector including under-the-table payments, procurement corruption, and medicine embezzlement.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM Manual to be updated with strengthened control framework. Budget guidelines and capacity development plan for internal audit to be prepared and adopted. Audit management software to be implemented. Contractual staff provided to internal audit director for quality and timely audits. Semiannual internal audits with follow-up mechanism. Fixed asset and inventory management software to be developed by December 2018 and populated by December 2019. Clear performance specifications and objective evaluation criteria for procurement. Procurement probity assurance providers engaged. Beneficial ownership disclosure required. Third-party monitoring for service provider verification. Timely and complete payment tracking. Disclosure of procurement results and financial data on MOPH website. Regular performance and financial audits. Implementation of MOPH anti-corruption strategy.

identified_risks.risk_description: Technical design risk: Weak oversight by MOPH of service providers; limited community accountability; inadequate understanding of contracting model; risk of overreporting by service providers when payments linked to service levels; poor governance and transparency throughout administrative chain; overcentralization of functions; inconsistent enforcement of guidelines; lack of clarity on responsibility and accountability between central and provincial levels; poor utilization of data for decision-making
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Functional review to clarify roles and responsibilities. Development of SOPs for service provider performance management. Training of MOPH contract managers and provincial staff. Performance-based contracts with fee-for-service component. Third-party verification of service provider reports on six-monthly basis with public disclosure. Semiannual performance reviews with involvement of provincial authorities. Link payments to outputs. Community health shura and CDC involvement in oversight. Guidelines development for community oversight role. Data warehouse development and capacity building for data analysis and use. User-friendly information dissemination within and outside MOPH.

identified_risks.risk_description: Sector strategies and policies risk: MOPH currently revising BPHS and possibly EPHS guidelines. Uncertainty about final scope and cost of revised packages. Need to maintain cost-effectiveness while expanding services.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: BPHS and EPHS will maintain focus on primary and essential health care interventions. Expansion will be cautious to maintain cost within reasonable limits. Project design based on proven cost-effective interventions aligned with SDG 3. Programmatic Advisory Services and Analytics (PASA) support to generate evidence on inefficiencies and innovations to inform future engagements and project adjustments.

identified_risks.risk_description: Health care waste management concerns: Different health care facilities not properly using color coding system for segregating, transporting, and disposing of medical wastes. Medical wastes mixed with municipal wastes with increased volume. Limited private sector services for collection and transport.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Updated Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Health Care Waste Management Plan (HCWMP) to be implemented in all identified facilities. ESMF and HCWMP mainstreamed during implementation through supervisions, meetings, M&E, reporting, and corrective actions. Safeguards points included in standard bidding documents and civil works contracts. Monitoring framework per ESMF and HCWMP. Climate change screening conducted with overall risk rated low for project implementation period (until mid-2021).

identified_risks.risk_description: Gender-based violence (GBV) widespread in Afghanistan with 1,669 reported cases annually. Women's access to health services constrained by geographical obstacles, cultural factors, and low female literacy. Undernutrition in children under five high (41% stunted, 10% wasted, 25% underweight). Critical health behaviors suboptimal with marginal change over 15 years.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Gender Gap Analysis conducted to inform interventions. Project supports: improving distribution and availability of female health workers through contract incentives and MOPH oversight; improving HIS gender indicators for disaggregated data; mainstreaming health sector response to GBV with improved reporting system; training and sensitization on GBV; measures to promote enabling environment and mitigate harassment at MOPH and facilities; strengthening Gender Department at MOPH. BPHS/EPHS providers to provide health response including referral and reporting for GBV survivors. Social and behavioral change communications campaigns on maternal health, nutrition, and family planning. Community engagement through CCAP and CDCs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple stakeholders with different priorities and interests. Power-sharing tensions in National Unity Government. Coordination challenges between central and provincial levels. Community engagement and accountability mechanisms need strengthening.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: High-Level Health Program Oversight Committee established with broad representation from MOPH, MOF, local governance, UN agencies, bilateral donors, and civil society. World Bank convening role to align multiple donors. Joint implementation reviews with development partners. Health Development Partners' Forum for coordination. Community engagement through CCAP and CDCs. Community scorecards for monitoring. Grievance redress mechanism strengthened for community voice. Semiannual performance reviews involving provincial authorities and stakeholders.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - MOPH has existing GRM but not widely used. Project strengthens GRM through: (1) BPHS/EPHS providers disseminating information about GRM in all health facilities and advertising through social media; (2) MOPH reviewing and improving SOPs for GRM and disseminating to all provinces; (3) Semiannual reviews to monitor grievance nature and prepare province-wise action plans; (4) Inclusion of GRM information in social and behavioral change communications campaigns. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for project-affected communities at http://www.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/products-and-services/grievance-redress-service. World Bank Inspection Panel available at www.inspectionpanel.org.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender is a priority focus area. Project supports: (1) Improving distribution and availability of female health workers across country through contract incentives and MOPH oversight; (2) Improving HIS gender indicators for gender-disaggregated data; (3) Mainstreaming health sector response to GBV with improved reporting system, training, and sensitization; (4) Measures to promote enabling environment and mitigate harassment at MOPH and health facilities; (5) Strengthening Gender Department at MOPH for technical support and monitoring. BPHS/EPHS providers provide health response including referral and reporting for GBV survivors. Social and behavioral change communications campaigns address maternal health, nutrition, and family planning. Gender tag applied with analysis of gender gaps, specific actions to address gaps, and monitoring indicators. Project beneficiaries include 38.5 million females expected to receive essential HNP services.
vulnerable_groups: Poor and lowest economic quintile populations (disproportionate project benefits through focus on PHCs, rural areas, and free BPHS care)
Women and girls (constrained access due to geographical, cultural, and literacy barriers; GBV survivors)
Children under five (high undernutrition rates: 41% stunted, 10% wasted, 25% underweight)
Internally displaced persons (over 200,000 displaced in first half of 2017)
Refugees (296,000 returned from Iran and Pakistan in first half of 2017)
Rural populations (concentrated poverty, limited service access)
Conflict-affected populations in southern and eastern provinces

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Document notes: 'The provision of BPHS and EPHS through contracting out in Afghanistan has become a model for other fragile states trying to rebuild their health system after emerging from conflict.' Afghanistan Health Services Study found 'remarkable resilience of health service delivery in conflict-affected areas' with no statistically significant differences in improvements between severely, moderately, and minimally conflict-affected provinces. Project design based on 15 years of experience implementing health services in conflict context. Lessons learned from ongoing SEHAT operation emphasize close work with local communities to mitigate security risks. Community engagement through CCAP and CDCs strengthens local ownership and accountability. Project supports delivery of essential health services as key tool to improve overall stability in country. Third-party monitoring includes assessment in all 34 provinces including conflict-affected areas.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces multiple fragility dimensions: (1) Security fragility: 2,640 killed and 5,380 injured January-September 2017; over 200,000 internally displaced in first half 2017; 296,000 refugees returned; security critical challenge in southern and eastern provinces; (2) Political fragility: Power-sharing tensions under National Unity Government; elections scheduled for 2019; potential political disruptions; (3) Economic fragility: Heavy aid dependency; economic growth only 2.6% (2017), 3.2% (2018), 3.6% (2020) vs. 9.6% average 2003-2012; poverty increased from 36% to 39.1%; unemployment 22.6%; (4) Institutional fragility: Highly uneven and thinly spread technical capacity; long-term aid dependency; weak governance and transparency; corruption concerns; (5) Social fragility: High maternal mortality (650 per 100,000 live births); high child mortality (55 per 1,000 under-five); high undernutrition; widespread GBV; low female literacy; suboptimal health behaviors. Project addresses fragility through strengthening health service delivery, institutional capacity, governance, and community accountability.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is critical implementation challenge, especially in southern and eastern provinces. Project mitigates security risks through: (1) Contracting-out model allowing service delivery through NGOs and non-state providers who may have better local access and relationships; (2) Close work with local communities as lesson from ongoing SEHAT operation; (3) Community engagement through CCAP and CDCs strengthening local ownership; (4) Third-party monitoring providing independent verification across all 34 provinces; (5) Community scorecards and grievance mechanisms enabling community voice; (6) Focus on primary health care and essential services that are resilient and can be delivered even in challenging security environments. Document notes health service delivery has shown 'remarkable resilience' in conflict-affected areas.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - significant displacement and refugee movements. Document states: 'Between January and September 2017, 2,640 people were killed and around 5,380 injured. The number of Internally Displaced People has increased at an alarming pace: in the first half of 2017 alone, more than 200,000 people have been displaced internally due to conflict and more than 296,000 refugees have returned from Iran and Pakistan.' Project addresses displacement through: (1) National coverage of all 34 provinces ensuring services available to displaced populations; (2) Focus on primary health care and essential services accessible to mobile populations; (3) Community health workers providing outreach and mobile services; (4) Free BPHS care reducing financial barriers for vulnerable populations; (5) Third-party monitoring and community accountability mechanisms ensuring services reach displaced and vulnerable groups; (6) No involuntary resettlement triggered by project (no new construction, only minor renovations within existing facility compounds).
